title: All-Purpose Mean Estimation over R: Optimal Sub-Gaussianity with Outlier Robustness and Low Moments Performance

reviews.summary: The paper studies the 1-D “single-δ” mean estimator of Lee & Valiant (2022) and proves four new properties: (A) robustness to adversarial data corruption in the strong contamination model with an additive σ√η term, (B) optimal high-probability rates in the low-moment regime z ∈ (1, 2), matching Devroye et al. (2016) up to constants, (C) neighborhood optimality (Dang et al., 2023) for any estimator that is both sub-Gaussian and adversarially robust (hence for the Lee–Valiant estimator), and (D) asymptotic normality and efficiency. The proofs extend the ψ-estimator and duality framework of Lee–Valiant to low-moment constraints and develop contamination-stability lemmas controlling the “influence parameter” α used by the estimator.
strengths: The paper substantially extends the theory around the Lee–Valiant estimator: (i) it provides, to my knowledge, the first adversarial-contamination analysis for this single-δ estimator; (ii) it generalizes the dual LP-based Chernoff-bounding technique to z-th moment constraints for z ∈ (1, 2); and (iii) it proves a general “robust + sub-Gaussian ⇒ neighborhood optimal” meta-result that cleanly answers an open question raised by Dang et al. (2023).

The low-moment extension is technically interesting: replacing a variance constraint by a z-th moment constraint within the duality framework is nontrivial, and the resulting rates (M_z)^{1/z} (log(1/δ)/n)^{1−1/z} are sharp up to constants with an explicit c_z.
The asymptotic normality/efficiency result is crisp and contrasts usefully with the known inefficiency of MoM.

The paper is a theory contribution; the results are nonasymptotic with explicit constants and careful probabilistic statements. The robustness lemmas precisely track how α shifts under η-corruption and how those shifts propagate to the estimator error.
The paper provides helpful intuition for the estimator’s structure (e.g., “weighted discarded mass” via α) and recaps the ψ-estimator reformulation that makes concentration arguments tractable.
The contamination analysis is modularized into interpretable lemmas (sandwiching α between nearby robustness levels; high-probability lower bounds on α in the clean case).

If accepted, the work further solidifies the Lee–Valiant estimator as a central object for 1-D robust mean estimation, now with guarantees spanning finite-variance, contamination, low-moment, beyond-worst-case optimality, and asymptotics.
The general Proposition that robust+sub-Gaussian implies neighborhood optimality is conceptually unifying and may carry over to other single-parameter estimators.
weaknesses: The adversarial robustness guarantees require η ≤ Θ((log(1/δ))/n), i.e., the number of outliers is O(log(1/δ)), independent of n. This is far more restrictive than the usual robust statistics regime allowing a constant η. Calling the estimator “robust to an η-fraction of corruption” without making this restriction prominent risks overstating the result.
The constants in the contamination error terms (e.g., 222, 135) are large; while the authors note they may be improved, this limits immediate practical interpretability.
No empirical evaluation is provided to substantiate the “all-purpose” claim or to examine finite-sample constants and tuning in practice (e.g., the tradeoff between δ and tolerated corruption).

There are several typos and notational slips likely from PDF extraction (e.g., “Suppose both log 1/n and δ are bounded,” where log(1/δ)/n is presumably intended; occasional garbled symbols). Some statements involving δ versus δ′ could be presented more cleanly.

The “dual use” of δ to control both confidence and contamination tolerance is an important and unusual feature; its practical implications (small η scaling with 1/n) should be stated prominently in Section 2 and the abstract to avoid confusion.

While MoM and trimmed mean are discussed, the paper could more explicitly position its contamination guarantees against other 1-D robust M-estimators (e.g., Catoni-type, Huber-type, recent winsorized/trimmed analyses) that handle constant η, even if they may not have optimal leading constants in the i.i.d. finite-variance setting.

The low-moment section claims a better constant than MoM across z ∈ (1, 2], but there is no numerical comparison of constants or discussion relative to recent sharp trimmed/winsorized results (e.g., Oliveira et al., 2025; other recent winsorized mean analyses).
reviews.questions:
1. The contamination results require η ≤ c (log(1/δ))/n, i.e., O(log(1/δ)) outliers. Can you clarify prominently (e.g., in the abstract and Section 2) that the estimator’s adversarial robustness is of this “few-outliers” type, and contrast it with methods that allow constant η?
2. Is there a principled modification of Step 2 (e.g., changing the right-hand side to scale with an η-estimate) that would allow robustness for constant η while preserving optimal clean-i.i.d. constants, perhaps at the cost of a multiple-δ or adaptive-δ scheme?
3. Practically, how would you recommend choosing δ when η is unknown? Can one adaptively increase the RHS in Step 2 until a stability criterion is met, while controlling the eventual failure probability δ′?
4. In Theorem 2.4, can you provide a short discussion or plot of c_z versus z, and explicitly show c_z → √2 as z → 2 to ease comparison with the finite-variance constant?
5. Could you include small-scale experiments comparing against MoM, trimmed, and winsorized means under (a) small η consistent with your theory and (b) larger η to illustrate the regime where the estimator’s robustness breaks down?
6. Several places seem to contain minor typos (e.g., “Suppose both log 1/n and δ are bounded ...”). Can you clarify these assumptions, especially where log(1/δ)/n smallness is needed?
7. The neighborhood optimality proposition requires success probability ≥ 1 − δ/2 under η-corruption; are there estimators of interest that satisfy the robust+sub-Gaussian precondition but fail neighborhood optimality for other reasons (e.g., randomized algorithms)? Any comments on the necessity of determinism or other conditions?
reviews.assessment: This paper delivers a strong suite of theoretical results around a prominent 1-D estimator: adversarial-outlier analysis (albeit for a vanishing fraction η ≲ (log(1/δ))/n), optimal low-moment rates, a general bridge to neighborhood optimality, and asymptotic normality/efficiency. The techniques extend the Lee–Valiant duality framework in a meaningful way and address questions raised in the recent beyond-worst-case literature. The main reservation is that the adversarial robustness is only to O(log(1/δ)) outliers—substantially weaker than constant-η guarantees that are standard in robust statistics—and this limitation is not emphasized enough relative to the “all-purpose” claim. The paper also lacks empirical evidence and has some clarity issues in statements and notation. On balance, I find the theoretical advances significant, but would prefer to see the robustness limitation more transparently framed and, ideally, some empirical validation. I lean toward a borderline recommendation; with a clearer presentation of the contamination regime and modest experiments, this could be a solid accept. As it stands, I recommend a weak reject.
